title: Branching and Loops
summary: Use labels, branch instructions, and conditional flags to implement loops and conditional logic in ARM assembly.
content_html:
Labels as Branch Points

Labels can mark the location of instructions (not just data). Since every instruction is loaded into memory and has an address, we can jump to any instruction by its address. This is called branching.

MOV R0, #0
Loop:
  ADD R0, R0, #1
  B Loop          ; Branch back to Loop (infinite loop)
HALT

In this example, 'Loop:' marks the address of the ADD instruction. 'B Loop' tells the Program Counter to jump back to that address.

The Program Counter (PC)

The CPU uses the Program Counter (PC = R15) to track which instruction to execute next. After each instruction, PC is automatically incremented. Branch instructions change PC directly.

Normal execution:  PC = PC + 4  (next word in memory)
After branch B X:  PC = address of label X

Unconditional Branch: B

B someplace   ; Jump to 'someplace' unconditionally

Conditional Branches

Conditional branches only execute if certain CPU status flags (in the CPSR) are set. Flags are set by comparison instructions (CMP) or arithmetic with 'S' suffix.

CMP Instruction

CMP R0, #10    ; Compare R0 with 10 (sets flags, discards result)
CMP R0, R1     ; Compare R0 with R1

Conditional Branch Instructions

BEQ label   ; Branch if Equal (Z flag set)
BNE label   ; Branch if Not Equal (Z flag clear)
BLT label   ; Branch if Less Than (signed)
BGT label   ; Branch if Greater Than (signed)
BLE label   ; Branch if Less Than or Equal
BGE label   ; Branch if Greater Than or Equal

Implementing a Loop

; Count from 0 to 9
MOV R0, #0         ; Counter = 0

Loop:
  ADD R0, R0, #1   ; Counter++
  CMP R0, #10      ; Compare with 10
  BNE Loop         ; If not 10, loop again

HALT               ; Counter = 10, stop

Implementing an If-Statement

; If R0 == 5, set R1 = 100
  CMP R0, #5
  BNE skip         ; If not equal, skip
  MOV R1, #100     ; R0 was 5, set R1
skip:
  ; Continue here

Branch and Link: BL

BL (Branch and Link) is used for function calls. It saves the return address in the Link Register (R14/LR) before branching.

BL myFunction    ; Jump to myFunction, LR = return address
...
myFunction:
  ; do something
  MOV PC, LR     ; Return: set PC = LR (or BX LR)

exercises.title: Program Counter role
exercises.prompt: What does the Program Counter (PC) contain?
options: The address of the next instruction to execute
The current result of the last calculation
exercises.answer: The address of the next instruction to execute

exercises.title: CMP instruction
options: Compares R0 with 10 and sets CPU flags
exercises.answer: Compares R0 with 10 and sets CPU flags

exercises.title: BL purpose
exercises.prompt: What does BL (Branch and Link) do differently from B?
options: Saves the return address in LR before branching
exercises.answer: Saves the return address in LR before branching

exercises.title: Returning from function
exercises.prompt: How do you return from a function in ARM assembly?

exercises.title: Infinite loop
exercises.prompt: Which code creates an infinite loop?

exercises.title: BNE
options: The Zero flag is NOT set (values were not equal)
exercises.answer: The Zero flag is NOT set (values were not equal)
